source_metadata.title_raw: James H. Wilkins - A-19-177 - January 7, 2020 - Fresno

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
James H. Wilkins 
6758 N. Willow Ave. 
Fresno, CA 93 710 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-177 
Dear Mr. Wilkins: 
January 7, 2020 
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Tulare (the City) 
and Councilmember Greg Nunley regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the Act) 1 and Section 1090. The Commission may provide advice only in regard to the 
Act and Section 1090, we express no opinion regarding any other laws that may apply including but 
not limited to common law conflict-of-interests provisions. Please note that we are not a finder of 
fact when rendering advice and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71) 
Under Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating 
to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Tulare County District Attorney's Office, 
which we have done. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )(3 ). ) We received a written response from the district 
attorney's office, a copy of which is enclosed in accordance with Section 1097.l(c)(4). We are also 
required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in 
a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
1. 
"Can Councilman Nunley, recognizing that the anticipated growth of the City will 
require a certain street to be widened which will eventually require the City to purchase property 
from the owner of the property adjacent to the existing street for the needed right of way, and as a 
city council member, propose and vote to approve the City widening said street, and then 
immediately after the matter has been approved, proceed to purchase the adjacent property?" 
2. 
"If he is prohibited from immediately purchasing the adjacent property, is there a 
time period that must expire before he would be allowed to purchase the adjacent property? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
FileNo. A-19-177 
Page No. 2 
1. 
No. Proposing a City project, then subsequently purchasing property adjacent to that 
project that the City must acquire for the project, would violate Section 1090(a). 
2. 
To the extent that Councilman Nunley proposes or votes to approve the street 
widening project, or participates in a proposal or vote, Section 1090 prohibits him from purchasing 
the property so long as the City's vote will necessitate the future purchase of the property by the 
City. There is no specified time-period that applies. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
Your request sets forth the following facts: 
Greg Nunley is a member of the City of Tulare City Council. In addition to serving as a 
council member, Mr. Nunley owns a construction and development company. This inquiry 
concerns his activities as a council member and his potential development activities. 
Specifically, can Councilman Nunley, recognizing that the anticipated growth of the City 
will require a certain street to be widened which will eventually require the City to purchase 
property from the owner of the property adjacent to the existing street for the needed right of 
way, and as a city council member, propose and vote to approve the City widening said 
street, and then immediately after the matter has been approved, proceed to purchase the 
adjacent property? If he is prohibited from immediately purchasing the adjacent property, is 
there a time period that must expire before he would be allowed to purchase the adjacent 
property? 
In response to supplemental questions, you stated that Councilman Nunley has not 
participated in any decisions related to a City street-widening project and has taken no action to 
enable his prospective purchase of the adjacent property. You were asked whether the City would 
take by eminent domain all or part of the adjacent property for the project if the street-widening 
project is approved. In response, you stated: "In large part by negotiated purchase." 
ANALYSIS 
A 
Section 1090. 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Making or participating in making a contract has been broadly construed to include 
instances where a public official has influence over the contract or its terms. (See 80 Ops. Cal. Atty. 
Gen. 41.) When members of a public board, commission or similar body have the power to execute 


FileNo. A-19-177 
Page No. 3 
contracts, each member is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of all contracts by 
his or her agency regardless of whether the member actually participates in the making of the 
contract. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 645 & 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County 
of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49 (2006).) 
B. 
Proposing a City Project, then Purchasing Property Adjacent to that Project, Would 
Violate Section 1090. 
Section 1090(a) states that city officers "shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members. Nor 
shall [they] be purchasers at any sale or vendors at any purchase made by them in their official 
capacity." 
In this case, you state that Councilman Nunley knows that: (1) the City's anticipated growth 
will require a City street to be widened; (2) to accomplish this, the City would need to initiate a 
street-widening project (the Project); and (3) the City would need to acquire private property 
adjacent to the Project. You state that the City would acquire property for the Project "in large part 
by negotiated purchase." 
A member of a city council is ineligible to make or assist in making a contract with his 
private employer, and a contract so made is void as against public policy. (Miller v. City of 
Martinez (1938) 28 Cal.App.2d 364, 368.) A contract is "made" by a public official for purposes of 
state conflict of interest statute ifhe had the opportunity to, and did, influence execution of the 
contract directly or indirectly to promote his personal interests; thus, a public official can violate the 
statute even though he did not participate in the contract's execution. (People v. Wong (2010) 113 
Cal.Rptr.3d 384, 397-397.) Officers of a governmental body may not exploit or prostitute their 
official position for their private benefit. (Terry v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 206.) 
Here, by proposing a project in his official capacity for the City, Councilman Nunley would 
be engaged in the "making" of a contract for the City to acquire property needed for the proposed 
project. Section 1090 would prohibit Councilman Nunley from subsequently acquiring a financial 
interest in property adjacent to the project that he proposed on the City's behalf, which would 
require the City to acquire the property from Councilman Nunley. 
The factual scenario presented here would involve the type of "self-dealing" in a contract by 
a public official that Section 1090 was enacted to prevent: 
Section 1090 of the Government Code codifies the common law prohibition and the general 
policy of this state against public officials having a personal interest in contracts they make 
in their official capacities. Mindful of the ancient adage, that "no man can serve two 
masters," "a self-evident truth, as trite and impregnable as the law of gravity," the section 
was enacted to insure that public officials "making" official contracts not be distracted by 
personal financial gain from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance to the best 
interest of the entity which they serve, and at least with respect to those contracts, it does so 
by removing or limiting the possibility of their being able to bring any direct or indirect 
personal influence to bear on an official decision regarding them. The mechanism of the 


File No. A-19-177 
Page No. 4 
section is one of prohibiting public officials from being personally financially interested as 
private individuals in any such contract .... 
(66 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 156, 157-158 (1983) [citations omitted].) 
Where a city official enters a contract to sell property to the city after proposing the project 
that required the contract, the fact that the contract occurs after the proposed project does not 
eliminate a potential Section 1090 violation. For instance, the Supreme Court has found that even 
an official's resignation from office does not absolve a Section 1090 violation were the official has 
been involved in the preliminary stages of the planning and negotiation process. (Stigall v. City of 
Taft, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 571.) Section 1090 also prohibited a councilmember from participating 
in the establishment of a loan program and then apply for a loan under the program after leaving 
office. (81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317,212 (1998).) 
Accordingly, proposing a City project and subsequently purchasing property adjacent to that 
project that the City must acquire for the project, would constitute "self-dealing" in a contract by a 
public official and would violate Section 1090(a) on its face. 2 Additionally, Councilmember Nunley 
is prohibited from purchasing the property after proposing or voting on the project, or participating 
in the proposal or vote, so long as the City's vote will necessitate the future purchase of the property 
by the City. The fact that any future purchase by the City will occur after the proposal of the project 
does not eliminate a potential Section 1090 violation, and there is no time-period that would apply 
to allow a City official to purchase the property after proposing the project.3 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
O-e.~ral -counsel ~ 
-
---
,., 
~ 
---
7 
<'.'.'.. 
-
( 
Jo~ 
, Fi ser Jr. 
0 nior Counsel, Legal Division 
JMFjr:aja 
Encl.: Letter from the Office of the District Attorney, County of Tulare, dated August 30, 2019 
3 Analysis of the requirements under the Act is unnecessary based on the effect of Section I 090 on the facts provided.

2.
